answer:
fix it, don't kill it

verdict: ltv:cac of 2.2 isn't dead but it's below the 3:1 benchmark for healthy saas. + payback is the real issue: $900 cac / $150 mo = 6 mo payback on revenue, ~7.5 mo assuming 80% gross margin. that's ok-ish (b2b norm is <12 mo) but ur profit per customer is only ~$1,100 lifetime — thin cushion if churn ticks up

why not kill: 2.2x means channel is gross-margin positive & google ads is usually fixable b4 it's scrapped. killing ur only paid channel at $6k/mo (~6-7 customers/mo) w/o replacement = growth stall

fix plan, in order of impact:
1. audit search terms report — kill broad match waste, add negatives. typical acct wastes 20-40% of spend here
2. cut to exact/phrase match on high-intent bottom-funnel kw only (competitor terms, "x software", "x tool for y")
3. check conversion tracking — optimize to qualified demos/trials or better, offline conversions synced from crm, not raw form fills. google optimizing to junk leads is the #1 silent cac killer
4. landing page ≠ homepage. dedicated lp per kw theme, 1 cta. even 20% cvr lift drops cac to ~$750
5. raise ltv side too: annual prepay discount, expansion revenue, or price test — $150/mo b2b is often underpriced
6. reallocate 20-30% of budget to test retargeting + 1 cheaper channel (linkedin organic, seo, cold outbound) so ur not single-channel dependent

target: cac <$650 (ltv:cac 3+, payback <5 mo). give fixes 60-90 days; if cac won't budge below ~$800 after that, then shift budget elsewhere

one caveat that changes the math: if ur ltv is growing (young cohorts, low churn) or these customers expand, 2.2 today may be 3+ tomorrow — check cohort retention b4 any drastic cuts
